reason: HITL approval is human-bound — the round-trip is dominated by operator attention, not runner latency. Cannot be benched synthetically.

measurement_methodology.instrumentation: the production HITL middleware emits two timestamps per approval: `t_approval_requested` (when the runner pauses for human input) and `t_approval_received` (when the operator clicks approve / deny in the UI).
measurement_methodology.collection_window: 30 days of production telemetry. Excludes denials (they're a different distribution) and excludes approvals on a HITL_TIMEOUT cleanup (they're not real operator decisions).
measurement_methodology.publication_format: median + IQR + p95 + p99 + long-tail max. Human attention is not Gaussian — the tail matters more than the mean.
measurement_methodology.comparable_segments: report separately by tier: (a) hot-keyboard operators reviewing inline, (b) async ops paged via Slack, (c) overnight queue items batch-reviewed in the morning. The tier mix changes the headline median by orders of magnitude.

what_this_is_not: A runner-side performance number. The runner enters and exits the HITL gate in <100 µs (measured separately in tool_dispatch + middleware deltas). The minutes-to-hours total is wall-clock waiting for a human, not compute.
next_action: Awaiting production telemetry sample. The first publishable cut needs a 30-day window of clean, instrumented operator traces.
